coredata.dc:title: A multifunctional carbon dots with near-infrared fluorescence for Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH detection and tumor diagnosis
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xiaojing
Wang, Yingying
Xu, Jingjing
Zhao, Mei-Xia
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have shown potential applications in fluorescence tracing and imaging due to their excellent optical and chemical properties. In this work, a new type of carbon dots (Cy7-CDs) with near-infrared absorption and emission was synthesized from cyanine dyes and polyethylene glycol by hydrothermal reaction. The prepared Cy7-CDs exhibited low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which were applied to detect the noble metal ions Au3+/Hg2+ and glutathione via the ‘on–off-on’ mode. In addition, in vivo fluorescence images demonstrated that Cy7-CDs could efficiently accumulate in the tumor. Therefore, Cy7-CDs could act as a multi-functional probe for biological sensing and tumor diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Near-infrared
Fluorescence
Noble metal ions
Probe
Tumor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH using Cy7-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21501044), Program for Innovative Research Talents from the University of Henan Province (18HASTIT049), Foundation of Science and Technology Department of Henan Province (182102410075), Major cultivation project of First-class discipline of Henan University (2019YLZDJL09), and Special Professor project of Henan University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of Cy7-CDs (10 µg/ml) solution with different concentrations of (a) Au3+ (0–45 μM) and (b) Hg2+ (0–45 μM), respectively. Insert: the linear relationship between F/F0 and the concentrations of Au3+/Hg2+. (F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of Cy7-CDs in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively; λex = 735 nm).
Fluorescence responses of Au3+@Cy7-CDs (a) and Hg2+@Cy7-CDs (b) to various concentrations of GSH (0–80 µM); λex = 735 nm.

Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells co-incubated with Mito Tracker Green and Cy7-CDs (10 µg/mL) (a1-a3), and then added Au3+ (b1-b3)/Hg2+ (c1-c3), respectively. HeLa cells pretreated with NEM (1 mM), and incubated with Cy7-CDs and Mito Tracker Green. Then Au3+ (d1-d3) /Hg2+ (e1-e3) were added, followed by GSH (2 mM) (f1-f3)/(g1-g3). From the left column to the right column: (a1-e1) blue channel (mitochondria staining), (a2-e2) NIR channel, and (a3-e3) overlay of the blue and NIR channels.

Fluorescence images of 4 T1 tumor-bearing mouse nude with intravenous injection of Cy7-CDs for different time (0, 2, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 24 h), and the fluorescence of main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kindey) and tumor.

head.ce:title: A multifunctional carbon dots with near-infrared fluorescence for Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH detection and tumor diagnosis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Natural Medicine and Immune-Engineering of Henan Province, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Natural Medicine and Immune-Engineering of Henan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Natural Medicine and Immune-Engineering of Henan Province, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have shown potential applications in fluorescence tracing and imaging due to their excellent optical and chemical properties. In this work, a new type of carbon dots (Cy7-CDs) with near-infrared absorption and emission was synthesized from cyanine dyes and polyethylene glycol by hydrothermal reaction. The prepared Cy7-CDs exhibited low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which were applied to detect the noble metal ions Au3+/Hg2+ and glutathione via the ‘on–off-on’ mode. In addition, in vivo fluorescence images demonstrated that Cy7-CDs could efficiently accumulate in the tumor. Therefore, Cy7-CDs could act as a multi-functional probe for biological sensing and tumor diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence imaging is an effective technique for tracing samples in living organisms, because of its outstanding advantages of high sensitivity, easy operation, and real-time detection. Many probes based on organic fluorescent dye have been developed, which have greatly promoted significant advance in biomolecular tracing and medical diagnostic imaging [1-3]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial, has attracted wide attention because of their unique advantages in chemical stability, excellent optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and so on in comparison with organic fluorescent dyes. The excellent properties of CDs make them ideal candidates for use in bio-related fields such as sensing [4-8], bioimaging [9,10], photothermal and photodynamic therapy [11,12], etc. Therefore, it is great significance to develop a multifunctional fluorescent nanosensor with high selectivity and sensitivity.

In recent years, many CDs with excellent optical properties have been synthesized using small organic molecules as carbon sources. There are abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs, which are widely used to detect metal ions and biomolecules [8,13]. For instance, new carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized using uric acid as raw material, which emit bright blue fluorescence.NCDs could sensitively and selectively detect Hg2+ and Ag+ ions accompanied by fluorescence quenching [14]. The quenching of CDs fluorescence by Fe3+ could be restored after the introduction of dopamine (DA). As a result, a simple “on–off-on” system was achieved for rapid detection of DA [15]. Red fluorescent CDs were synthesized from neutral red and citric acid by Zhou group, which was successfully used to sense Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+ in cells and in zebrafish [16]. Overall, a series of fluorescent CDs for the detection of metal ions and biomolecules had been reported. However, to the best of our knowledge, the fluorescence of CDs was almost in the visible region, and the near-infrared fluorescent CDs have not been reported. Near-infrared fluorescence (NIR) (650–900 nm) has the advantages of minimal damage to biological samples, low background interference, and deep tissue penetration. Probes with NIR emission are preferable for biological detection and fluorescence imaging in living organisms [17,18].

Heavy metal ions are toxic, and pose a serious threat to the environment and human health. Au3+ and Hg2+ are common heavy metal ions that can damage many organs and immune systems in humans even at very low concentrations [19-21]. In addition, reduced glutathione (GSH) is a critical intracellular antioxidant, which maintains redox homeostasis in cells and plays important roles in many physiological and pathological aspects [22,23]. Abnormal GSH levels will lead to variety of diseases such as cancer, Parkinson disease, Alzheimer’s disease, etc [24,25]. Moreover, tumor-targetedNIRfluorescentsensors can realize the tumor diagnosis. Therefore, the development of multifunctional CDs with NIR fluorescence will help to understand the function of biomolecules and diagnose related diseases in vivo.

Herein, we synthesized new carbon dots Cy7-CDs by hydrothermal reaction using cyanine dyes and PEG as raw materials. Cy7-CDs displayed near-infrared absorption and fluorescence spectrum, excellent photostability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. Cy7-CDs were applied to detect Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH via an “on–off-on” mode. Cell imaging proved that Cy7-CDs could target mitochondria and detect analytes with high selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, the fluorescence distribution in mice indicated that Cy7-CDs could be selectively enriched at tumor sites and act as a fluorescent imaging agent for tumor diagnosis.

Cy7-Cl was synthesized in our laboratory. PEG400 were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Analyte solutions of KCl, CaCl2, NaCl, MgCl6·H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, CdCl2, AlCl3, ZnCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, PbCl2, HgCl2, AuCl3, NaCN, NaNO2, H2O2, NaClO, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, NaHS, Na2S, Hcy, Cys, and GSH were prepared by direct dissolution of proper amounts of chemicals, which were all obtained from Energy Chemical. Mito Tracker Green FM, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma Chemical Company. PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 50 mM) was prepared in our laboratory. The chemicals and reagents were used without further purification.

1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were detected on Bruker AVANCE III. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Cy7-CDs were measured on a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100). UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra were recorded using spectrophotometer UV-3600 Plus. Fluorescence spectra were obtained from a Perkin Elmer LS-55 fluorimeter. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were measured on a VERTEX 70 (Bruker, Germany). Confocal microscope images were performed with Ziss 880. The fluorescence tracing experiment was carried out on Perkin Elmer IVIS Lumina III.

2-[4-Chloro-7-(1-ethyl-3,3-dimethyl(indolin-2-ylidene)]-3,5-(propane-1,3-diyl)-1,3,5-heptatrie n-1-yl)-1-ethyl-3,3-dimethyl-3H-indolium (Cy7-Cl) was synthesized according to the previous articles [26]. The synthesis of Cy7-CDs was as follows: Cy7-Cl (5 mg) and PEG400 (100 mg) were dissolved in 5 mL anhydrous ethanol, and mixed for 10 min in ultrasonic field. The mixture was then put into a Teflon-linedhydrothermal autoclave reactorand heated at 160℃ for 4 h. After the reaction, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was further transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000) and dialyzed for 48 h. Finally, the dark brown solid Cy7-CDs were obtained by freeze drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH using Cy7-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cy7-CDs was dispersed in the PBS buffer solution (50 mM PBS, pH 7.4), the different concentrations of Au3+ (0–45 μM) were separately added into 2.5 mL of 10 µg/mL Cy7-CDs solution. The fluorescence spectra were recorded with excitation wavelength at 770 nm. Hg2+ ions were detected by the same method as above. The selectivity experiment of Cy7-CDs were conducted by adding a certain amount of metal ions (including K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Au3+ and Hg2+). Cy7-CDs solution (10 µg/mL) was pretreated with Au3+ (45 μM), and different concentrations of GSH (0–80 µM) was further added to detect the fluorescence intensity changes at the excitation wavelength of 770 nm. The selectivity of Au3+@Cy7-CDs were performed by adding CN–, NO2 –, H2O2, ClO-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, HS-, S2-, Hcy, Cys, and GSH to the Au3+@Cy7-CDs solutions, respectively. All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

MTT method was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of Cy7-CDs. HeLa cells were seed in 96-well plates at density of 4500 cells per well and incubated at 37℃ for 24 h. Then the medium was replaced separately by different concentrations (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 µg/mL) of Cy7-CDs, and then cultured for 48 h. MTT solution (50 µL, 1 mg/mL) was added into each well, and removed after 4 h of incubation. Afterward, 100 µL DMSO was then added in each well, and the plate was gently shaken for 10 min. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured using a microplate reader.

HeLa cells in the logarithmic growth phase were seeded in the glass dish and cultured for 24 h. Then, the Cy7-CDs solution (10 µg/mL) was added into each dish, after incubation for 10 min, the cells were washed with PBS for three times to remove free Cy7-CDs that did not entered the cells. Subsequently, Mito TrackerGreen FM (10 µg/mL) was further added for mitochondrial staining and washed three times with PBS. Finally, fluorescence images were recorded using confocal microscope.

The Balb/c mouce of 15–20 g were subcutaneously injected with 4 T1 (2 × 107) cells in the right anterior axilla. After seven days of feeding, 5 mg/kg Cy7-CDs was injected intravenously into the tail. Fluorescence imaging of mice was performed at 2, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 24 h, respectively. In the end, the mouse was sacrificed and the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumor were collected and analyzed.

The structure of Cy7-Cl was characterized by 1H NMR and 13C NMR (Fig. S10 and Fig. S11). The structure and optical properties of Cy7-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), FT-IR, ultraviolet–visible-near infrared (UV–Vis-NIR) spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectrometer.

The morphology characterization of Cy7-CDs was measured by TEM as displayed in Fig.S2. Cy7-CDs were evenly dispersed and uniform, with an average diameter of 4 nm. The FT-IR spectra were investigated to identify the surface chemical groups of Cy7-CDs (Fig. S3). The FT-IR spectra of Cy7-CDs exhibited absorption bands of stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H at 3428 cm−1, the absorption centered at 2926 cm−1 was assigned to stretching and bending vibrations of CH3 and CH2, and the peak at 1635 cm−1 was attribute to absorption bands of C = C. Besides, the C-O vibrations at 1253 cm−1 and the bending stretching of C-N at 1402 cm−1 were observed. The FT-IR spectra of Cy7-CDs exhibited absorption peak of bending stretching of C-N at 1402 cm−1 [27], the absorption centered at 2926 cm−1 was assigned to stretching and bending vibrations of CH3 and CH2, and the peak at 1635 cm−1 was attribute to absorption bands of C = C. Besides, the peaks around 3428 and 1253 cm−1 were consistent with the stretching vibrations of O–H and C-O-C [28], illustrating the existence of abundant oxygen-containing groups in the composition of Cy7-CDs. Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of Cy7-CDs and Cy7-Cl, the bonds of O–H and C-O-C appeared in the Cy7-CDs, illustrating that O was successfully doped into Cy7-CDs. These abundant surface groups contribute to the excellent solubility of Cy7-CDs.

The UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra of Cy7-CDs in aqueous solution were used to investigate their optical properties. As can be seen from Fig. S2, the absorption spectrum of Cy7-CDs ranges from 600 to 900 nm, and the maximum absorption peak is 770 nm. Subsequent fluorescence characterization in Fig. S4 revealed that the fluorescence intensity of Cy7-CDs gradually increased with the excitation wavelength changing from 690 nm to 770 nm, and the emission peak remained nearly unchanged (λem = 795 nm). The excitation-independent emission behavior indicated that the Cy7-CDs were well-passivated and uniform [10,29].

The abundant of functional groups on the surface of Cy7-CDs inspired us to investigate their potential sensing applications. The selective experimentswereconducted to analyze the selectivity of Cy7-CDs for metal ions. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence response of Cy7-CDs were investigated in the present of various metal ions (including K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Au3+). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of Cy7-CDs was effectively quenched by Au3+/Hg2+ ion, while the other metal ions caused slight or negligible fluorescence quenching. Titration experiments were carried out to test the selectivity of Cy7-CDs toward Au3+/Hg2+ in detail.Increasingtheconcentration of Au3+ woulddecrease the fluorescence intensity of Cy7-CDs (Fig. 1 a). The fluorescent signal was almost completely quenched when Au3+ concentration was 45 µM. A good linear relationship can be obtained between the value of F/F0 and the concentration of Au3+ (0–45 µM). A similar trend was observed when Cy7-CDs solution was added with different concentrations of Hg2+ ions (0–45 µM) (Fig. 1b). The detection limits of Au3+ and Hg2+ were estimated to 0.33 µM (Detection limit = 3σ/k). The fluorescence responses of Cy7-CDs to Au3+/Hg2+ could be completed in about 4 min (Fig. S7).

Cy7-CDs chelated with Au3+/Hg2+, resulting the formation of the complex Au3+@Cy7-CDs and Hg2+@Cy7-CDs. The reversibility of Au3+@Cy7-CDs was investigated in the present of common biological substances including CN−, H2O2, ClO-, HS-, S2-, SO3 2-, Cys and GSH as shown in Fig. S5. After the addition of GSH, the fluorescence quenching of Cy7-CDs by Au3+ recovered completely, while other substances did not restore the fluorescence of Cy7-CDs. The results demonstrated that Au3+@Cy7-CDs possessed the selectivity for GSH over other substances, and GSH have strong favor for Au3+ ions and tend to drag them off from the complexes. Thus, Au3+@Cy7-CDs could be used as an “off–on” type NIR probe for GSH.

Titration investigation showed that the fluorescence intensity of Au3+@Cy7-CDs displayed steady enhancement with increasing the concentrations of GSH. After 70 µM GSH was employed, the fluorescence signal was completely recovered, which was close to the fluorescence spectra of Cy7-CDs. The values of F/F0 exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of GSH (Fig. 2 ). The detection limit of GSH was 0.63 μM. Similarly, when Hg2+ was added, the fluorescence quenching of Cy7-CDs was observed, but the fluorescence intensity partially recovered after further addition of GSH.

As shown in Fig. S8, Cy7-CDs response to Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH via an “on–off-on” mode. Upon added with Au3+ ions, the NIR fluorescence of Cy7-CDs turn off. After further addition of GSH, Au3+ combine with GSH and fell off from the complex, and the fluorescence emission of Cy7-CDs is restored.

The fluorescence intensity at different pH values was also performed. It can be seen that Cy7-CDs and their complexes are stable in a very wide range of pH values (2–11). The emission of Cy7-CDs was strongest in physiological pHs of 6–8 (Fig. S6). After chelating with Au3+/Hg2+, the fluorescence of Cy7-CDs was quenched in the pH range of 2–11. The results demonstrated that Cy7-CDs could be used to detect Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH in physiological environment. Moreover, the cytotoxicity of Cy7-CDs was investigated to evaluate the suitability for biological application. The results of the MTT assay showed that survival rates of the cells exceed 70% after incubation with Cy7-CDs at low concentrations (0–10 µg/mL) for 48 h (Fig. S9), confirming that Cy7-CDs are suitable for bioimaging applications.

It was reported that cyanine dyes incline to locate at mitochondria [30-32]. Moreover, GSH was reported to be involved in alleviating oxidative stress and balancing redox homeostasis in mitochondria [33]. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate whether Cy-CDs can anchor mitochondria and monitor precious metal ions and GSH in living cells.

Fluorescence imaging of living cells was carried out as shown in Fig. 3 , the HeLa cells incubated with Cy7-CDs exhibited strong NIR fluorescence when exited at 770 nm. The merged images clearly displayed bright yellow fluorescence, suggesting that Cy7-CDs were efficiently internalized into the mitochondria. When Au3+/Hg2+ ions were further added, Cy7-CDs still emitted bright NIR fluorescence, indicating that Cy7-CDs did not chelate with the metal ions, which may be related to the high expression of GSH in tumor cells. In subsequent experiments, HeLa cells were pretreated with N-methylmaleimide (NEM, a thiol-blocking agent) before other substances were added. As expected, almost no NIR fluorescence was observed in Fig. 3d and 3e. GSH was further added into the ‘off’ mode system caused by Au3+/Hg2+ (Fig. 3f and 3 g), and the fluorescence of Cy7-CDs was re-emitted, realizing intracellular ‘on–off-on’ mode. The results demonstrated that Cy7-CDs could target mitochondria and be used as a probe for monitoring Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH in vivo.

Furthermore, 4 T1 tumor-bearingmice model was used to assess the Cy7-CDs as probe for tumor imaging. The mice were injected with Cy7-CDs from the tail vein at a dose of 5 mg/kg, and then the whole-body fluorescence distribution was detected at different time points (Fig. 4 ). Clear fluorescence signal was observed in the tumor region after 10 h of blood circulation, and remained obvious fluorescence signals at the tumor site even at 48 h. Finally, the mouse was sacrificed, and the main organs and the tumor were collected. Ex vivo imaging showed that remarkable fluorescence signal was observed in the tumor, while no obvious fluorescence signal was detected in the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kindey), confirming that Cy7-CDs could effectively accumulate in the tumor site. Cy7-CDs exhibits preferable distribution in tumor, which may be due to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [34,35].

In conclusion, a novel NIR carbon dots Cy7-CDs was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Cy7-CDs exhibited good hydrophilicity and low toxicity. Cy7-CDs were applied to detect Au3+/Hg2+ and GSH, and the corresponding LODs were 0.33 and 0.63 µM. Confocal microscopy analyses indicated that Cy7-CDs could target mitochondria, selectively chelate with metal ions and further react with GSH via the ‘on–off-on’ mode. In addition, bioimaging confirmed that Cy7-CDs could efficiently accumulate in the tumor. This work provides a simple approach to prepare a multi-functional probe for biological sensing and tumor diagnosis.
Xiaojing Yang: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yingying Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Jingjing Xu: Investigation. Mei-Xia Zhao: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21501044), Program for Innovative Research Talents from the University of Henan Province (18HASTIT049), Foundation of Science and Technology Department of Henan Province (182102410075), Major cultivation project of First-class discipline of Henan University (2019YLZDJL09), and Special Professor project of Henan University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small-molecule fluorescent probes for imaging and detection of reactive oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur species in biological systems
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Jiao, Y. Li, J. Niu, X. Xie, X. Wang, Small-molecule fluorescent probes for imaging and detection of reactive oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur species in biological systems, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 533−555.

Chem. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.W. Liu, X.X. Hu, K. Li, Y. Liu, Q. Rong, A mitochondrial-targeted prodrug for NIR imaging guided and synergetic NIR photodynamic-chemocancer therapy, Chem. Sci. 8 (2017) 7689−7695.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel porphyrin-based near-infrared fluorescent probe for hypochlorite detection and its application in vitro and in vivo
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, J. Min, W. Wang, Y. Wang, A novel porphyrin-based near-infrared fluorescent probe for hypochlorite detection and its applicationin vitroandin vivo, Analyst 143 (2018) 2641–2647.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications
Green Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.L. Liu, B.B. Chen, C.M. Li, C.Z. Huang, Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications, Green Chem. 21 (2019) 449–471.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mohapatra, S. Sahu, N. Sinha, S.K. Bhutia, Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells, Analyst 140 (2015) 1221–1228.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An inner filter effect based sensor of tetracycline hydrochloride as developed by loading photoluminescent carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers
Nanoscale

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel carbon dots with dual excitation for imaging and silve in living cells
Dyes Pigm.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Guo, S. Ye, H. Li, J. Song, J. Qu, Novel carbon dots with dual excitation for imaging and silve in living cells, Dyes Pigm. 183 (2020) 108723.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One pot synthesis of highly fluorescent N doped C-dots and used as fluorescent probe detection for Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution
Sens. Actuators, B

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue fluorescent folate-receptor-targeting carbon dots for crvical cancer cellular and tissue imaging
Mater. Sci. Eng., C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Li, J. Jiang, Y. Yana, Red, green, and blue fluorescent folate-receptor-targeting carbon dots for crvical cancer cellular and tissue imaging, Mater. Sci. Eng., C 93 (2018) 1054−1063.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of red-emitting carbon dots from pulp-free lemon juice for bioimaging
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, Y. Ji, J.-S. Wei, Q.-Y. Gao, Z.-Y. Zhou, H.-M. Xiong, Facile synthesis of red-emitting carbon dots from pulp-free lemon juice for bioimaging, J. Mater. Chem. B 5 (2017) 5272–5277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot to synthesize multifunctional carbon dots for near infrared fluorescence imaging and photothermal cancer therapy
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zheng, Y. Li, S. Liu, W. Wang, Z. Xie, X. Jing, One-pot to synthesize multifunctional carbon dots for near infrared fluorescence imaging and photothermal cancer therapy, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2016) 23533−23541.

Adv. Mater.

J. Mater. Chem. B

Chem-Eur. J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A near-infrared fluorescent probe for sulfide detection
